game.event: From-Position replay — continued from move 17 of emgosr vs Maia 800

engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: You had mate in five — and reached for a quiet move instead
mistakes.explanation: You were a full queen up with Black’s king boxed on d8, and there was a forced mate here. The cleanest is the engine’s 36.Qxf7, mate in five (36…Qc5 37.Qe8+ Kc7 38.Ba6 and the net closes). But you didn’t need to see the whole line: 36.Rxc7 — a move 30% of players at your level find — also forces mate by ripping away the last defender of the back rank. Instead 36.Bb6 just improved a bishop and gave the king room to breathe. Still winning (+9.7), but the forced finish was gone and the game dragged on another 26 moves.

takeaways.lesson: When you’re up a queen, hunt for the forcing move before the tidy one.
takeaways.detail: Before every move in a won position, ask one question first: is there a check or capture that starts a forced sequence? Calculate that line to the end before you even consider a quiet improving move.

takeaways.lesson: Bring your heaviest pieces to the enemy king — don’t reposition minor pieces.
takeaways.detail: Queens and rooks deliver mate; a bishop shuffle almost never does. Point your rooks at the file or rank the enemy king is sitting on and keep them there.

mistakes.title: A check that chased the king in a circle, not the check that mated
mistakes.explanation: With 40.Ba6+ you gave a check — but after 40…Kd7 the king simply stepped back and you had gained nothing; the evaluation stayed around +16 and the position stalled. The engine’s 40.Bxd6+ — a move more than half your peers find (56%) — is a check that also removes the d6 pawn and tears open the king, mating in five (40…Kd8 41.Nb6 and it’s over). Same bishop, one square different, and the difference is the whole game: one check wins a pawn and mates, the other just says “check.”

takeaways.lesson: Only give a check if you’re better after the king moves.
takeaways.detail: Before any check, picture the king’s reply and ask ‘am I actually improving?’ A check the king answers for free is just a lost tempo that lets it escape.

takeaways.lesson: Prefer checks that also capture or open a line toward the king.
takeaways.detail: Bxd6+ removed a defender and a pawn shield in a single move. Rank your checks by what they destroy, not by the fact that they’re legal.
